edges.evidence: Chive and garlic chives are both mild allium garnishes with overlapping dumpling and noodle roles.
Garlic and ginger form the universal aromatic base of Asian stir-fry and curry.
Garlic and rosemary are paired under roast lamb, chicken, and root vegetables.
Garlic and chicken are the foundational aromatic-protein pair in global roast and braise cookery.
Garlic and pork loin define roast pork, lomo al ajillo, and pan-seared cutlet bases.
Garlic and chicken breast are paired in roast chicken, piccata, and pan sauces worldwide.
Garlic and onion are the universal paired aromatics in Mediterranean and global savory cooking.
Garlic and shallot build layered allium depth in pan sauces and compound butters.
Ramp delivers the same garlic-onion allium base when wild garlic is unavailable in spring cooking.
Garlic chives substitute for chive where a slightly stronger allium garnish is acceptable.
Leek and onion combine in potato-leek soup and braised white vegetable preparations.
Leek and celery share light aromatic roles in stock and poaching aromatics.
Onion and leek are interchangeable softening aromatics in soups and braises with mild sulfur sweetness.
Scallion and leek both provide green allium freshness in Asian and European savory cooking.
Leek white and light green substitute for onion in soups and braises requiring gentle sweetness.
Onion and thyme share stock, roast poultry bed, and French aromatic bundles.
Onion accompanies braised beef brisket in barbecue and Jewish holiday preparations.
Onion and chicken share classic stock, roast bed, and pan sauce composition.
Onion and celery are paired aromatics in mirepoix, stuffing, and soup bases worldwide.
Onion and carrot form the base of mirepoix and soffritto in European savory cooking.
Shallot and onion share allium aromatic base character in sauces, stocks, and pan deglazes.
Yellow onion substitutes for shallot in everyday cooking when shallots are unavailable.
Garlic with scallion substitutes for seasonal ramps in pesto and spring sautés.
Scallion and ginger are paired in Chinese and Japanese aromatic stir-fry bases.
Scallion and garlic are paired in ginger-scallion stir-fry and noodle aromatics.
Scallion green tops substitute for shallot as a fresh allium finish in Asian and fusion dishes.
Shallot and garlic are classic paired aromatics in French and Southeast Asian bases.
Shallot is the standard substitute for onion in refined pan sauces when a milder allium is needed.
Artichoke and feta combine in Greek aginares and Mediterranean mezze platters.
Artichoke and garlic define Roman carciofi alla romana and Spanish tapas.
Arugula and watercress both bring peppery leaf bite to salads and fish garnishes.
Asparagus and tarragon are classic partners in béarnaise and spring vegetable service.
Asparagus and chicken breast are paired in spring bistro and grill platters.
Asparagus and garlic are paired in spring sautés and grill preparations.
Bitter melon and garlic are paired in Filipino and Chinese bitter gourd stir-fry.
Bok choy and garlic are standard partners in Chinese stir-fry and steamed greens.
Bok choy and tatsoi are mild Asian brassicas for quick wok and soup applications.
Bok choy substitutes for napa cabbage in stir-fry when stalk crunch is preferred.
Broccoli and Parmigiano are paired in Italian contorno and pasta primavera.
Broccoli and garlic are a standard pairing in Italian and Chinese vegetable cookery.
Broccoli and cauliflower are brassica florets roasted, steamed, and puréed with parallel texture.
Broccoli and rapini share Italian brassica floret character with garlic and chili service.
Broccoli substitutes for cauliflower when greener brassica flavor is acceptable.
Brussels sprouts and garlic are paired in roast and pan-seared autumn side dishes.
Cabbage and onion are foundational in slaw, borscht aromatics, and stuffed cabbage.
Cabbage and carrot compose classic coleslaw and Eastern European pickle bases.
Green cabbage and napa cabbage are interchangeable shredded brassicas for slaw and fermentation.
Cauliflower and garlic anchor roast cauliflower and Sicilian agrodolce preparations.
Cauliflower substitutes for broccoli in roast and cheese-gratin applications.
Celery and clams are essential partners in New England chowder and vongole aromatics.
Celery and fennel bulb both contribute aromatic crunch to mirepoix and raw salad preparations.
Celery and celeriac are the same species expressed as stalk and root with shared aromatic profile.
Kale and collard greens are sturdy brassica leaves for long-simmered Southern and Mediterranean greens.
Collard greens substitute for kale in long-braised greens with smoked meat.

Cucumber and tomato are paired in Greek horiatiki and Middle Eastern salad.
Dandelion and mustard greens are bitter braising greens with assertive leaf character.
Lettuce and endive compose crisp salad bases with complementary mild and bitter leaf character.
Endive and radicchio are chicory-family leaves used for bitter salad and grill applications.
Fennel and salmon are classic partners in poached and roasted fish preparations.

Fennel and onion are paired in Italian soffritto and roast fish bed preparations.
Fennel and celery combine as aromatic bed vegetables under roast poultry and pork.
Garden pea and snow pea share Pisum sativum lineage in spring vegetable cookery.
Shelled garden pea substitutes for snow pea pods in spring risotto and purée.
Green bean and tomato are classic partners in Italian contorno and Greek fasolakia.
Green bean and snow pea are crisp pod vegetables in stir-fry and blanched side applications.
Green bean substitutes for snow pea in Niçoise and haricots verts applications.
Kale and pork loin are paired in Portuguese caldo verde and American farm-table service.
Kale and garlic define Tuscan cavolo nero and Portuguese caldo verde flavor base.
Kale and mustard greens share bold brassica bitterness in pot liquor and stir-fry contexts.
Kale substitutes for collard greens in Tuscan ribollita and minestrone.

Okra and bell pepper share Creole and West African vegetable stew composition.
Okra and tomato are paired in gumbo, bhindi masala, and Creole stew.
Rapini and garlic with chili define southern Italian orecchiette accompaniment.
Broccoli substitutes for rapini when bitter broccoli-rabe character is not required.
Snow pea substitutes for green bean in stir-fry when flat pod texture is preferred.

Spinach and chicken breast combine in Florentine and stuffed breast preparations.
Spinach and garlic are paired in Greek horta, Catalan espinacs, and wilted greens.
Spinach and Swiss chard are tender leafy greens wilted interchangeably in sautés and pies.
Spinach substitutes for Swiss chard when a more tender leaf is preferred in sautés.
Swiss chard substitutes for spinach in wilted greens and spanakopita-style fillings.
Tatsoi substitutes for bok choy in Asian greens with a spoon-shaped leaf.
Water spinach and garlic define Southeast Asian kangkong stir-fry.
Anaheim and poblano are long mild green chiles for roasting and chile relleno.
Roast bell pepper and Parmigiano finish peperonata and antipasto vegetable service.
Bell pepper and onion are paired in fajitas, peperonata, and stir-fry trinity.
Bell pepper and tomato combine in sofrito, shakshuka, and stuffed pepper fillings.
Bell pepper and poblano are mild capsicum peppers for roast, stuffed, and fajita service.

Eggplant and Parmigiano finish melanzane and Italian gratin preparations.
Eggplant and basil combine in caponata and Mediterranean layered vegetable dishes.
Eggplant and salmon combine in Mediterranean composed plates with shared olive-oil cookery.
Eggplant and garlic anchor Mediterranean dips including baba ghanoush and melitzanosalata.
Eggplant and bell pepper share ajvar and Mediterranean grill vegetable platters.
Eggplant and tomato are canonical partners in caponata, ratatouille, and moussaka.
Jalapeño and cumin combine in Tex-Mex chili and spiced bean preparations.
Jalapeño and tomato define fresh salsa and pico de gallo in Mexican cuisine.

Jalapeño substitutes for serrano as a milder fresh chile in pico de gallo.
Poblano and onion are standard partners in chiles rellenos and rajas con crema.
Bell pepper substitutes for poblano in stuffed pepper dishes without roast chile depth.
Serrano substitutes for jalapeño when higher heat and finer dice are desired in salsa.
Tomatillo and cilantro are essential partners in salsa verde and Mexican green sauces.
Tomatillo and onion are essential partners in salsa verde and enchilada sauce.
Tomatillo and jalapeño combine in green salsa with shared Mexican sauce identity.
Tomato and tomatillo are tart nightshade fruits in salsa and sauce contexts.
Tomato and mozzarella define Caprese — the canonical tomato-cheese ingredient pair.
Tomato and Parmigiano-Reggiano finish pasta, risotto, and Caprese service.
Tomato and basil are the defining ingredient pair of Caprese and Italian summer cuisine.
Tomato and oregano anchor pizza sauce and Greek horiatiki seasoning.
Tomato and salmon share Mediterranean and Nordic poached fish preparations.
Tomato and black pepper are foundational seasoning partners in global sauce cookery.
Tomato and garlic define Italian and Spanish sauce bases from marinara to gambas al ajillo.
Tomato and onion are foundational partners in soffritto, salsa, and Mediterranean stews.
Tomato acidity mirrors high-acid Sauvignon Blanc in Mediterranean ingredient pairing literature.
Acorn and delicata squash are small-format winter squashes for roast and half-moon service.
Acorn squash substitutes for butternut in half-roast service with similar bake time.
Roast beet and feta are a stable salad and mezze pairing in Mediterranean cuisine.
Beet and onion are paired in borscht and Eastern European pickle and salad traditions.
Beet and carrot combine in roast root medleys and Eastern European salad.
Burdock and salsify are long roots braised or kinpira-style with earthy depth.
Butternut squash and rosemary are paired in autumn roast and soup preparations.

Butternut and kabocha squash are dense sweet squashes for soup, curry, and roast.
Carrot and parsley combine in bouquet garni, tabbouleh-adjacent salads, and garnish.
Carrot and braised beef share pot roast and borscht-adjacent stew composition.
Carrot and lamb leg share classic roast bed and navarin stew composition.
Carrot and celery combine with onion as the canonical three-part aromatic base.
Carrot and parsnip are sweet root vegetables roasted and puréed in parallel autumn preparations.
Carrot substitutes for parsnip when brighter color and milder sweetness are needed.
Celery substitutes for celeriac in mirepoix when root is unavailable — same aromatic family.
Delicata substitutes for acorn squash in roast wedges with edible skin.
Hubbard squash and onion combine in baked squash and harvest stew.
Hubbard and pumpkin are large Cucurbita squashes for purée and baking applications.
Lotus root and jicama are crisp watery roots for raw slaw and stir-fry crunch.
Kabocha substitutes for butternut in soup and tempura with chestnut-like density.
Parsnip and onion are paired in roast root trays and winter soup bases.

Pumpkin and onion are paired in soup, curry, and roast squash dishes.

Salsify and scorzonera are European roots with artichoke-adjacent savoriness in cream sauces.
Sunchokes and garlic are paired in roast and purée with shared earthy savor.
Turnip and carrot share English and Irish root stew and mash compositions.

Zucchini and thyme are paired in Provençal tian and roast summer vegetables.
